figures.caption: Table 3. Phase-1 vs Phase-2 reconstruction performance under different train–test protocols (mean ± std over 5 random seeds).
Table 7. Detailed architecture of the proposed CAE. Input size: 1× 1280× 872.

Figure 1. Overview of the Two-Phase Training Strategy. Phase 1 warms up the encoder with global structural similarity. Phase 2 freezes the global structure and refines the ROI using localized intensity (L1) and gradient (Sobel) losses, balanced via gradient calibration.

Figure 8. Latent space visualizations for the Hospital-1/2 → Hospital-3 split (frozen encoder), with points colored by hospital site. (a) PCA projections for Phase-1 (left) and Phase-2 (right) show strong overlap across hospitals and no major change in global latent geometry after refinement. (b) UMAP projections for Phase-1 (left) and Phase-2 (right) reveal site-dependent manifold structure, with held-out Hospital-3 samples occupying partially distinct regions, indicating observable domain shift. Phase-2 refinement preserves this structure without collapsing or artificially aligning domains.

Figure 3. The proposed CAE. The encoder maps the input to a 128- D latent vector z via global average pooling and a linear head. This compact z serves as the shared representation for reconstruction and downstream tasks.
Table 9. Phase-2 loss components evaluated on the held-out Hospital-3 test set (report-only; not used for loss selection)

Figure 4. Qualitative example on the held-out Hospital-3 test set. Left: original ultrasound image. Right: Phase-2 reconstruction. The NT region (green box) shows improved boundary preservation, consistent with the ROI Edge-MAE reductions reported in Table 3.
